speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm interviewing Mr. Henry Oster.

speaker: interviewee
text: The working conditions in Auschwitz on this animal farm, if you can call it, provided us also with the additional, I can't say freedom, but opportunity to move about. Because we were on this farm-like setting on these stables, that we were not in the camp to be selected or to be necessarily cruelly treated if and when the Germans decided to do something for their amusement, or to clean out, or select people for extermination, which was very frequently done due to the fact that everybody suffered from enormous malnutrition. And many people rapidly were declining. And as soon as you got too weak to do any other kind of work, you were gone. But when we came back at night, we had to be accounted for. And so at 6 o'clock every night, we had a head count. Well, my job, along with some others, was also to take the horses to be fed, not in the stable but on the fields. And we had to do field work. We had to do some plowing as long as our horses could do-- many different things. The one time I almost came to a bitter end was that I was taking this one horse, which was supposed to be ready for delivery of it's foal. But the Germans decided the horse needed the best nutrition. And I'm supposed to take it to a clover field where the horse could eat. Well, the horse decided at about five minutes to 6:00 to have its baby. And it laid down and proceeded to give birth just behind a bulge in, in the field. Of course, I knelt down to assist it, which we were told before and we had before, when I hear whistles and all kinds of commotion because I'm not there and neither is the horse for the head count, for the appell. I looked over the-- this bulge in the field. And all I could see, Germans with guns drawn, German Shepherds, again, searching for me and the horse. Now I-- I thought because they don't ask questions. You know, shoot first and then ask later. There was no explanation. One person-- no names or such thing didn't exist, no numbers, just one missing. And they went out on the field because there was not much of a chance to be anywhere else. It was a fairly confined area. I found, happily, a stick. And I raised my cap. And sure enough, you know, shots rang out. If my head had been in there, that would be no interview today. But I yelled in German, this horse is giving birth. You know, I'm here, I'm here. You know, I am doing what I'm supposed to do. Well, the first thing that was strange is Germans did not expect to hear German language. There were not many German-speaking prisoners. And so they weren't sure whether or not it's an officer, another soldier, or what. But when they came over this bluff, if you want to call it that, you know, they raised the gun, and because here I was in my striped uniform. But I was able to explain it. And they saw the foal half out of its mother. So that kind of saved my life. As it turns out, I had to go through two evening selections with one Mengele, and the other one somebody else who was always interested in, in youngsters for experiments. The camp I was in in Auschwitz was famous for medical experiments because he was there. There were experiments done for high altitude flying, for people freezing, all kinds of things. Fortunately, none of us 131 was chosen for any of these experiments. And, interestingly enough, every one of the 131 passed both inspections. I don't know if he was favorably inclined towards younger people. We, we don't know. Maybe he tried to save us for some other things. But we were not selected. My good fortune, so to speak, of speaking German make me stand out. And they make me a runner. A runner was from the stables with some documentation that we have a full head count, whatever it is. I have no idea what the-- going to the German administration. And the one time that I had a-- the only kindness I was ever shown, I had to pass the camp bakery, which was, of course, being manned by inmates. And they saw me walking by, and kind of surreptitiously, dropped a loaf of bread, which I proceeded to scoot up there and steal. Unfortunately, I got caught. The, the man in charge of the bakery caught me. They yank me in and proceeded to beat the living daylights out of me till I finally said something in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he, he stopped. And he says, but you speak German. And I said, yeah. I was born in Germany. I said, where? I said, in Cologne. He said, oh, my wife is from Cologne.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, having stopped the beating, he wasn't quite sure what to do. I was bleeding a little bit. It wasn't the worst beating one could have. But it was sufficient for-- it's a good beginning, as far as he was concerned, to teach a lesson. And he yelled and screamed at me. And he kicked me out. And you-- there were two or three steps that I fell down because I got a major, major kick. There was no way to control it. But surprisingly enough, the loaf of bread and one tomato followed-- the only kind I ever received in all these years. I went to make my report, which I had the papers there. Because he realized I had a, a mission. And it was the day, which was the most horrible that one could think of, where the trucks of the night's previous night's selection-- was waiting to go to Birkenau to be exterminated. And I had to walk between those two. Now here I was bloody. And I had a loaf of bread under my arm, which I was hiding under my uniform, making me probably look a little bit less emaciated, squeezing and holding on to the tomato. And nobody, strangely enough, even questioned me whether I, I do not belong on either one of these trucks. And that was probably the most horrifying moment of pure innocence, you know, where your life was threatened by the circumstances in which you found yourself. We stayed there for quite some time, for, well, a long time, doing the work, not having any idea-- by now, it's 1944-- that anything had been invaded. We had no idea that there was anything politically, militarily. Nobody has absolutely no knowledge. A few rumors here and there, but your rumors are mostly about survival. We were put with 20 other youngsters into a neighboring camp called Plawy. This was in January of-- no, this was October, 1944-- yeah, October, '44. And we were supposed to build that little neighboring camp into something which we never did understand. We worked. We did-- we worked on fields. There was-- there seemed to be no rhyme and reason. The one thing you notice, that the bunks you were on were all brand new, was all clean and new. So something was in the works that we never came to understand. We did different types of work with lumber. And, and you can never quite figure out what's the purpose for which we're here, which made everybody a little bit nervous. We were told in the middle of January that we have to be on a collection place. And we will have to leave. And I find myself with many of others, not all 131 on them. We were all separated. And it was disjointed, on what's finally considered to be a death march. It was wintertime. It was snow. We had about 1,200 people assembled. And we marched off out of the camp under heavy guard with a very simple rule. Whoever falls behind and creates a distance between the column and yourself will be shot. They had the jeep, like a personnel vehicle, with a machine gun, and officers, and soldiers. And you were heavily guarded. Not that you really had a chance to, to run anywhere, because you didn't have the strength. You didn't have any direction. You were certainly skinny, and recognized, and numbered, no hair. I mean, there was no escape. And off on the road, you had corpses by the dozen-- unbelievable. There was an intimidating concern that you had not to end at the end of the column. And it produced a remarkable speed. Because as everybody came behind, they now speed up. They want to be in front. And it's like a centipede. You just-- and you marched pretty rapidly. We did lose quite a few people. Some of them just gave up. They just had no strength to go on. The place was-- I mean, the distance we covered with one rest stop in the schoolhouse was atrocious. We ended up on the railroad platform once again. And this time, we were packed in. And I do mean packed in. There was only standing room. You could hardly breathe. You were face to face. We had people that finally passed away, died. They gave up or gave out. They didn't even sink. They didn't even fall down. We were guarded, because they were open wagons, cattle wagons. We were guarded on each wagon by a German soldier with a machine gun pointing down into it, as if we were going to escape. You couldn't even move to get out. But the unfortunate thing, Allied fighter planes had come by and had mistaken this for a troop carrier because they saw-- and started strafing the whole train. And we had quite a few casualties. I happened to, by coincidence, be in the corner. And so I had a, a two-sided protection. But bullets could have gone through that very easily. It was a-- well, a nightmare isn't the word. We came to another camp which was pronounced closed and could not be unloaded. And we proceeded. And we were on that train for six full days before we were unloaded. We could hardly make it. Of the 1,200, I believe it was 360 survivors. It was considered to be about less than one third. We couldn't-- I couldn't straighten out. I could hardly walk. We had been standing all the time. And when we finally did get out and managed to creep, and I do mean creep, they said, welcome to Buchenwald. And I was back in Germany-- made one incredible round trip. Buchenwald had been a facility that, that was sort of the privileged camp for political prisoners, for many, many German prisoners that had criminal records, political prisoners from other countries, Leon Blum from, you know, from France, who was Jewish was there, and so on. And there wasn't the direct killing going on when I arrived, because it was near the end the war. You did have, of course, thousands of people die-- hundreds basically each day by, I guess you might say, for, for choice of a better word, attrition. It's all, of course, malnutrition, rather than systematic killing. That had already kind of stopped. We did have the infamous Ilse Koch, who was the commandant's wife, riding through the camp. And she was responsible for tattooed skins being made into lampshades. And you do have the specimen. And they do have the shrunken heads. These things were still going on to a lesser degree, because we didn't know that Germany had been invaded, that Europe had even been invaded by the Allies. We had no such information. And from January to April, without going through the atrocities would say that the Germans did as a last act in trying to destroy the camp. We waited and had no activities, very little food. Most of us did recover from the little bread that they gave us to sustain us sufficiently. And then, for reasons that we did not know, we heard rumbling. We heard noise. We were forced to go to Weimar, which was the nearest city down the hill, to clean up the rubble of the destruction of the city. And that's the only clue we had that obviously, you know, there's still bombing going on. But the camp itself wasn't bombed. At previous time before I had arrived, it was, because they were making bullets there. But that had ceased because the factory was destroyed. So we lingered around, other than going down to Weimar, basically with the hope that in the rubble, you might find some extra food. The work was excruciating, because it was 10, 12 hours a day then to transport back up. And all of the sudden, things got kind of quiet. And for 11 days, 10 or 11 days, no food, no nothing. All of us were now much too weak. Tremendous death rate of people with this. For whatever reason, the younger inmates had a little bit more life-sustaining strength. We all had a great deal of hope. And one day while we were laying around, since you couldn't move you were so weak, we hear strange noises and some yelling. And we thought for sure the Germans are now eradicating this whole camp. You hear some shots, you hear some-- but you hear some joyous-- it wasn't anger, fear, panic, didn't know. We raised ourselves up and climbed up. The, the Germans did take potshots at us. But that's what we thought. We looked out. And the first thing somebody said, and we thought he was a hallucinating, there was a tank facing, coming through the building with a Star of David on it. Well, you figure, you know, that somebody has lost it. We looked. And that's what it was. General Patton had sent tanks up the hill, which Buchenwald was on a high mountain-- no other way to get up there except one railroad facility and a small street. And he had sent tanks up. And on the tank, he allowed a Yiddish, Polish, whatever speaking soldier to be there, which was a high risk position to be in. And the only way that they could make any sense out of them not being attacked-- and being misrecognized, is right in front, they painted the Star of David. And so we realized that were being liberated. We were liberated on the 11th of April, 1945, as I said, by Patton's Third Army. And we could not do anything to help ourselves. We were too weak. Well, it was immediately followed-- the troops were followed by the Red Cross. And they took those that seemed to have the best chance of survival. It's-- in the medical field, it's called triaging. Those that were too far gone, to give them help was of little value. So the ones that looked a little stronger, which turned out to be the younger ones, they put us into the, the barracks that the German SS, the guards, had occupied. And they gave us some minor food, milk. And we were-- hungry isn't the word. And we're all complaining that we're not getting enough food now that it's available. The tragedy was that we lost over 3,000 people because of food after the liberation. We were too weak to participate. But others were strong enough to go down. There were some Russian prisoners of war, which were given a little bit more food. So they a little bit more strength than the rest of us. And they had also put away certain things. There was all kinds of things that they could organize. They just went down the hill, mountain, brought back cattle, slaughtered it, cooked it in the kitchen, which did exist. And everybody had a feast and promptly died from the inability to absorb food. And again, very fortunate, the younger ones, having been taken care of by the Red Cross, ended up getting very little food because they sort of gradually fed us, not to have the tragedy of dying because of inability to assimilate food into our bodies, which had been undernourished for years, and in the last 10 or so days, totally incapacitated to make any kind of absorption of the food. So these people died because of food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To me personally, we were now in a terrible position. We had been liberated, something I had looked forward to for four and 1/2 years. And the next thing is a new worry. What next? What to do? Well, the Red Cross took names and disseminated the list of survivors all over the world. Jewish organization, the German-speaking paper in Los Angeles called the And they quickly listed the survivors that they found in all the camps. We didn't know what would happen. I refused to go back to Germany. I had no way-- I did not-- the slightest idea what to do. So we hung around, literally. We were planning something. We were going back to the-- to Poland and to-- one fellow was from Austria, Hungary, a lot of them. But you just didn't know what to do. The Red Cross said, you know, the juveniles, of which there were 400, will be divided between England, France, and Switzerland, and no particular choice given. It just-- I was designated to go to France.

speaker: interviewer
text: So quite some time later-- well, not quite some-- I have the dates somewhere. I was taken with many others to France. And the French Government provided an orphanage and made the effort to find any possibility of surviving family members to reunite. I personally never found anybody. I had a family of 19 family members. They all perished.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I was sitting in France. Now, France didn't seem to be a future, not that I had enough sense to figure out what my future would be. And I had volunteered, one of the few times I did, to go on the exodus to Palestine. I wanted to get out of Europe. And I was assigned to it. And two weeks before I was to leave, one of the newspapers had been seen by an uncle who lived in Los Angeles. And they made arrangement that I should come to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived in Los Angeles on what date?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in April 20 of 1946. I was privileged to have the, sort of, opportunity to sit in a cafe. I mean, big shots, you know, kids were sitting in a cafe in Paris. And since I did speak German and one other fellows from Lithuania spoke German-- he didn't pick Yiddish up very well-- a neighboring couple looked over. And they thought it was strange we have not yet grown our hair back. It was our first opportunity. And we, were were looking the little stubbles that were growing. This was, of course, still in, in early '45-- I mean, middle '45, when I had already known that my family had found me in the newspaper and-- in, in the listing. And we were discussing, because I was very excited. And this gentleman asked the waiter to bring me over. And he happened to be from Germany, Jewish, lives in France for a long time, was in the Ministry of Transportation. I mean, the coincidence on a cafe encounter-- and he was able to provide me with the first ship to leave Europe with civilians on it after the war to come to the United States. So I've been very lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what have you done since being in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came to the United States in 1946. And I, to my great surprise, had a new challenge. I knew, how do you do, thank you, and OK. So I came here. My family, of course, received me with all the capabilities that they had, which were very meager. They came, of course, as I said, in 1939 themselves. And they said, well, now you have to go to school. Well, I lived in Hollywood. And with three phrases of English-- it's about half a dozen words-- you couldn't get very far. I was very apprehensive to go to school now. And the oddity was that I was 18 years old, the time that everybody graduates. And here, this lug had to go to high school at an age that everybody already has completed, with no language skill.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your--

speaker: interviewee
text: The school had a foreign student adjustment class, which provided intensive English so that you could, you know, sort of catch up. And I did that. I was fortunate enough that the school had an incredible mixture, intermingling of different ethnic groups. And I picked up the language relatively quickly and ended up in the first 10% of the class, which allowed me to enter the University of California school system, UCLA. And I attended there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now I thought, what am I going to do there? Well, the first thing was that I encountered anti-Semitism, which was very disappointing. I had gone to a dentist for quite some time because I had terrible dental problems because of malnutrition. And I thought, well, this would be a nice profession to enter. And maybe if I'm lucky enough, and can get good grades, and concentrate, and devote myself, I can make something of myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as it turns out-- and I won't mention the school, it's local-- they had a quota system. And the dental school was not open to Jews by qualifications but only by what you could do for the school, bribery, all kinds of underhanded things. And I was very disappointed, very dejected Here, I prepared myself. I had gotten a degree. I went an extra year. My family thought that would always be very helpful. And as it turned out, I had a friend at UCLA that wanted to be an optometrist. I did not know what an optometrist does, or was, or anything. He, he told me. And when I was last rejected at the local university-- and it wasn't UCLA of course-- that it was by, again, coincidence that the school of optometry was the adjoining building to the dental school. I walked in there. And I was accepted, because I apparently had qualified. But once I was at the Southern California College of Optometry, I found out that I was in the first class-- this is 1954-- with an unrestricted [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] ethnic, religious, let's say, restraint. There was no restraint. Up to that time, you also could not get into that school unless you were white Protestant. Not even Catholics could get in very well. Of course, that has changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are now an optometrist?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I am an optometrist. I practice here in Beverly Hills. I taught at the college for 25 years. Again, because of some religious significance, that I was only the third Jew on the faculty, which they had asked, which I thought was kind of privileged, I guess, on the day of graduation. Normally, there was a time that you have to be out of college before you could join the faculty. And I taught there for 25 years, one day a week. And that's been my story.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you had to leave a message or tell the world something from your experiences, what would you want them to  know?

speaker: interviewee
text: The message is basically quite universal. The simple purpose for doing this, for going through this, rehashing all of this, it happens again. Right now, the future, it happens. People are people. People are cruel. People are adamant of wanting it their way. And their way is, if it's condoned by religious fervor, political, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it will happen. I have the conviction that, given any opportunity that people are willing to take with nobody safeguarding, this is not an isolated incident. Right now, well, we'll see this in the future, might hear about Bosnia, Herzegovina, Rwanda. People will let it happen. That's the only message. That's the only message anybody can give to anybody who cares to listen. Because people all over the world, whether it's Nazis or whether it's anybody else, if they believe that they can get away with something that's inhumane and serves their purpose, there will be countries, there will be people, there will be circumstances that will allow it to happen. And whether it's by education, or common sense, or by any kind of ideology, this is not something that was. It's something that currently is, and inevitably, sometime in the future will be again. And the message is to be on guard. You have a freedom, in this country at least, that allows you the freedom of speech, freedom of thought. But it must be directed in a constructive manner, not in idiotic, fanatical, and unreasonable things without some checks. And the problem is that we have different societies. We have different philosophies. And if some minority, in most cases, feels that there is a way to get away with it, they will at least try. And if you have enough people that will adhere to this with no safeguard, no police, no court, no judges opposing it because they believe in this false ideology, it will get out of hand. And it will repeat itself throughout history time and time again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Oster, thank you very much--

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: For sharing your story. I know you have some items that we would like to share with us as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.
